title: Stable Static Attention Routing for Orthogonal ELM Transformers
abstract: We introduce the Orthogonal ELM Transformer, a novel architecture that leverages the Isometry Property of orthogonal projections to create a stable static attention routing space. By initializing and freezing the Query (Q) and Key (K) projection matrices as orthogonal random matrices while keeping the Value (V) projection and Feed-Forward Networks trainable, our model significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters while maintaining expressive power. This design addresses the computational inefficiency and instability issues found in traditional Transformers, offering a more scalable and robust solution for a wide range of natural language processing tasks.
problem_framing: We reframe the challenge of maintaining stable and efficient attention mechanisms in Transformers by introducing a semi-random, strongly-constrained approach. Traditional Transformers rely on fully trainable attention matrices, which can lead to overfitting and computational inefficiency. Our Orthogonal ELM Transformer leverages the Isometry Property of orthogonal projections to create a stable static attention routing space, ensuring that the model remains expressive while significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters.
gap_pattern: Current methods for attention mechanisms in Transformers fail because they require a large number of trainable parameters, leading to increased computational costs and potential instability during training. These issues hinder the scalability and robustness of Transformer models, particularly in resource-constrained environments. By contrast, our approach transforms the attention mechanism into a semi-random, strongly-constrained space, addressing these limitations.
solution: Our approach transforms the attention mechanism in Transformers by introducing a semi-random, strongly-constrained design. The Query (Q) and Key (K) projection matrices are initialized as orthogonal random matrices and frozen during training, leveraging the Isometry Property to ensure a stable static attention routing space. Meanwhile, the Value (V) projection and the Feed-Forward Networks remain trainable, allowing the model to maintain its expressive power. This hybrid approach not only reduces the computational burden but also enhances the model's stability and efficiency, making it suitable for a variety of natural language processing tasks.
method_skeleton: Step 1: Initialize the Query (Q) and Key (K) projection matrices as orthogonal random matrices; Step 2: Freeze the Q and K matrices during training to maintain the Isometry Property; Step 3: Keep the Value (V) projection and Feed-Forward Networks trainable to preserve model expressiveness; Step 4: Utilize the stable static attention routing space to reduce computational costs; Step 5: Evaluate the model's performance on benchmark NLP tasks to demonstrate efficiency and stability.
innovation_claims: Transform the attention mechanism in Transformers from a fully trainable to a semi-random, strongly-constrained design by initializing and freezing the Query (Q) and Key (K) projection matrices as orthogonal random matrices, thereby reducing computational costs while maintaining model stability and expressiveness.

Reframe the Isometry Property of orthogonal projections as a key element in creating a stable static attention routing space, which significantly enhances the efficiency and robustness of Transformer models, particularly in resource-constrained environments.

Transform the balance between model expressiveness and parameter efficiency by keeping the Value (V) projection and Feed-Forward Networks trainable, ensuring that the model can adapt and learn effectively while leveraging the benefits of orthogonal projections.
experiments_plan: We will evaluate the Orthogonal ELM Transformer on several benchmark NLP tasks, including machine translation, text classification, and language modeling. The experiments will compare our model's performance with traditional Transformers in terms of computational efficiency, stability, and accuracy.
